title: Decentralized Exchange: The Uniswap Automated Market Maker
abstract: Uniswap is a system of smart contracts on the Ethereum blockchain and is the largest decentralized exchange with a liquidity balance worth up to 4 billion USD and daily trading volume of up to 7 billion USD. It is a new model of liquidity provision, so-called automated market making. For this new market form, we characterize equilibrium in the liquidity pools. We collect all 95.8 million Uniswap interactions and compare this automated market maker (AMM) to a centralized limit order book. We document absence of long-lived arbitrage opportunities, and show conditions under which the AMM dominates a limit order market.

article.introduction:
Uniswap is a decentralized exchange(DEX) that launched in November 2018. One of the striking features of this exchange is that, instead of a centralized limit order book, it pioneered a novel form of liquidity provision—automated market making. In such markets, liquidity providers do not explicitly set prices, but rather passively supply liquidity. Price impact for liquidity demanders is calculated mechanically. In spite of this, Uniswap has been very successful. To date, committed liquidity supply tops 4 billion USD across multiple cryptocurrencies and facilitates transactions up to 7 billion USD per day.
In an automated market maker (AMM) such as Uniswap, each asset pair comprises a distinct pool or market. Agents supply liquidity by adding the pair in proportion to the existing pool. Agents demand liquidity by adding one asset and removing the other. The relative proportion of the two traded assets determines the average price paid, and is calculated according to a predetermined downward-sloping, convex relationship. This is referred to as a bonding curve. It is surprising that a market in which liquidity suppliers do not explicitly control prices has viable liquidity supply and is attractive to liquidity demanders.
To analyze this new market form, we collect a novel, detailed data set of 95.8 million interactions with the Uniswap family of smart contracts. Our observations are at the block frequency and include the ordering sequence within each block. Using these data we can identify all flows into and out of 105,098 liquidity pools, that is, changes in liquidity supply as well as all of the token trades. We can therefore trace how liquidity is both supplied and demanded for each set of asset pairs and the payoffs to participants.
To structure our analysis, we focus on three key differences between an AMM and a limit order market. First, in the AMM, liquidity suppliers are not in competition as all payoffs are shared pro rata. In contrast, in a limit order book, strategic liquidity suppliers actively compete with each other as the costs and benefits of supplying liquidity are individual to each liquidity supplier. Second, in the AMM, price impact is determined by the bonding curve and is perfectly predictable given the size of the liquidity pool and the size of the incoming order. In contrast, in the limit order market, liquidity suppliers choose to post at a price that maximizes their profits and may even withdraw liquidity strategically. Third, in the AMM, liquidity provision and price discovery are separate and provided by different agents who collect different rewards. Liquidity suppliers in the AMM rely on outside arbitrageurs to set the correct price at which the liquidity is provided. They receive trading fee revenue while arbitrageurs get the trading profits.
We first characterize the effect of these three key differences in liquidity provision for an asset whose value is volatile. Risk-neutral liquidity suppliers, a liquidity demander, and an arbitrageur all interact. Arbitrageurs are strategic traders who profit from stale prices. Liquidity suppliers may be adversely selected as liquidity is posted before any potential asset innovation. We compare strategic liquidity provision in a stylized limit order market to an AMM.
In the stylized limit order book market, competing liquidity suppliers post prices to trade off adverse selection risk against profitable liquidity supply. Of course, if two liquidity suppliers are competing in the same market, each earns zero in expectation. As a result, each liquidity supplier has an incentive to invest to find trading opportunities in which he does not have to compete. This captures the idea that liquidity is cheap if two suppliers are competing on price. However, if they compete on other dimensions such as speed, the cost of liquidity may increase.
In the AMM, a liquidity supplier deposits a pair of assets into a pool in exchange for a liquidity token. On redemption, the liquidity token returns both assets but in their current proportion. The direct payoff to supplying liquidity is in the form of fees, and the indirect cost of supplying liquidity is the loss of committed capital if an arbitrageur has traded against the pool. This occurs if the relative price of one of the asset shifts, and an arbitrageur finds it profitable to buy the underpriced asset. The arbitrageur effectively rebalances the liquidity suppliers' portfolio at disadvantageous terms.
Our key insight is that the equilibrium size of a pool balances the fee revenue against this “picking-off risk.” Specifically, equilibrium is reached through a change in the size of a pool rather than a change in price because larger pools mechanically have a smaller price impact. If picking-off risk is high, the equilibrium size of the pool is small because small pools have a higher impact for the same size order. So, faced with a small pool, a rational arbitrageur optimally restricts their trade size, which mitigates the extent to which liquidity suppliers' portfolios are adversely rebalanced. A contribution of our paper is thus characterizing the equilibrium size of the liquidity pools as a function of asset fundamentals. Understanding theoretically and empirically the assets for which AMMs are effective is important as more venues experiment with this new market form.
Because of differences in market design between a limit order book and an AMM, gains from trade are split differently across market participants. In the limit order market, liquidity suppliers retain all of the price impact revenue from supplying liquidity, whereas in the AMM, arbitrageurs obtain this benefit while AMM liquidity suppliers earn only the fees. This differentially affects agents' incentives to supply liquidity on the AMM or in a limit order market. Further, in the limit order market, the fact that there can be both competition that decreases transaction costs and competition that increases transaction costs means that it does not always dominate the AMM for the liquidity trader. Indeed, for assets that have lower volatility (and hence adverse selection), the AMM can be more effective (i.e., is cheaper) at providing liquidity. This observation depends on the fundamental parameters of the tokens traded.
Our extensive data allow us to make four robust empirical observations. First, we document an equilibrium pool size, consistent with our characterization: Pools are larger the lower the volatility of the token price and the higher the level of liquidity trade. Second, we demonstrate that the AMM provides stable liquidity in comparison to a limit order market during an extreme market event. As we document, during extreme market events, both trading fee revenue and gas fees increase. Thus, liquidity providers obtain more revenue and face higher costs to withdraw during market turmoil. Both channels stabilize liquidity supply. Third, consistent with our model, we find that price impact on Uniswap is small with low volatility, while price impact on Binance is higher and exhibits high volatility. Consistent with the predictions of our model, we show that price impact on Uniswap is smaller relative to Binance for low token volatility, medium trade sizes, and high noise trading. Interestingly, in March 2022, Binance integrated Pancake Swap, a Uniswap clone, into the Binance ecosystem and as a result made their trading venue more similar to an AMM. In the data, we subsequently observe a dramatic drop in observed price impact on Binance as their trading system moved closer to an AMM. Fourth, we document that gas fee differentials between centralized exchanges (CEXs) and DEXs are negligible for traders who want to keep custody of their tokens.
A large literature studies liquidity provision in limit order markets. Since Glosten (1994), the efficiency of the limit order book in supplying liquidity has been widely accepted. Most modern markets operate as a form of an open electronic limit order book. Thus, the success of the AMM is surprising. In Glosten (1994), the payoff to the marginal liquidity provider at any price balances price concession from liquidity demanders against picking-off risk. Thus, no competing exchange could offer liquidity at that price and still make profits. As we show, in AMMs, due to pro rata sharing of profits, equilibrium is driven instead by the average liquidity provider. Further, liquidity providers trade off trading fees against a type of picking-off risk. The size of the pool adjusts, and the bonding curve shifts out until the market reaches equilibrium.
As noted, the payoffs to liquidity provision are shared pro rata in AMMs. Although time priority (or first in, first out queuing) is standard practice in most equity markets, there has been little research to examine the optimality of this precedence rule. Glosten (1998) is a rare exception. He shows that pro rata rationing changes the marginal payoff to liquidity provision and hence the posted volume. Ceteris paribus, we expect pro rata rationing to increase liquidity supply. Using a natural experiment from the Treasury Futures market, Haynes and Onur (2020) find that under pro rata rationing, while order sizes and profitability of later submissions are higher, price efficiency is lower. We note that in contrast to equity markets, option markets have long used pro rata rationing, first to allocate marketable orders across market makers and second to allocate early exercise assignments across writers. Hao, Kalay, and Mayhew (2009) document such rationing in options markets.
More recently, the rise of high-frequency traders (HFTs) has generated research into competition that does not lead to cheaper liquidity. Biais, Foucault, and Moinas (2015) present a nuanced view of the effect of speed on market competition as it generates both positive and negative externalities. Empirically, Brogaard, Hendershott, and Riordan (2019) examine the limit orders submitted by HFT on a Canadian exchange. They document high limit order submission and cancellation (95% of the message traffic), which is consistent with strategic liquidity provision.
A few papers have analyzed the theoretical properties of constant function market makers. In a general framework, Angeris and Chitra (2020) show that this class of mechanisms can reflect “true” prices. They also provide a bound on the minimum value of assets held by such an automated system. These two concepts are related because of the increasing price impact faced by a potential arbitrageur. Further, Angeris et al. (2021) present a more specific analysis of Uniswap, while Park (2021) highlights blockchain-related costs inherent in AMMs. Aoyagi (2020) characterizes the effect of information asymmetry on these types of markets and shows that the equilibrium liquidity supply size is stable. More recently, Ito and Aoyagi (2021) present a model in which an informed trader chooses between a centralized “market maker” market and an AMM market. By contrast, we characterize the endogenous liquidity supply for each set of markets and we solve for the optimal venue for a liquidity trader. We also discuss why, in general, the tokens traded on a CEX are not substitutes for tokens traded on a custodial (centralized) exchange.
Most closely related to our work is Capponi and Jia (2021). They present a model of and test for an AMM with a focus on competition among arbitrageurs. This competition allows the authors to consider the joint determination of gas fees and pool size. By contrast, our focus is on the comparison of a limit order market with AMM as markets for liquidity. In particular, we view the gas fees as an important precommitment mechanism not to withdraw liquidity from the AMM. This allows us to focus on the tradeoff between liquidity fees and adverse selection. We also note that our data are more extensive. A more recent working paper by Barbon and Ranaldo (2021) compares transaction costs and price efficiency on various decentralized exchanges and Binance for five different token pairs. They conclude that due to the fixed cost of gas fees, trading costs on the DEX are roughly comparable to the CEX. Our detailed blockchain data allow for a much richer investigation across multiple tokens.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIdescribes in detail constant product AMMs, SectionIIpresents the model, SectionIIIpresents our sample and provides some stylized facts, SectionIVcharacterizes the equilibrium pool size, and SectionVcompares trading costs on the AMM to the limit order market. Finally SectionVIconcludes.
article.I. Detailed Description of Constant Prod:
In this section, we describe the market making mechanics on Uniswap V2 for readers unfamiliar with the protocol. We present an additional numerical example in AppendixE. Our model is detailed in SectionIIbelow.
Providing Liquidity. Each swap pool comprises a pair of cryptocurrencies. Most frequently, as we document below, one of the currencies is ETH, the native cryptocurrency on the Ethereum Blockchain. Expositionally, we typically use ETH as the numéraire, and refer to the other generic coin as the “token.” An agent wishing to provide liquidity to their preferred pool deposits equal amounts of ETH and the token into the pool. The deposit ratio of ETH to token is determined by the existing ratio in the pool, which implicitly defines the ETH price of the token.
An agent who makes such a deposit receives a proportional amount of a liquidity token. This third token is specific to the pool and represents an individual liquidity provider's share of the total liquidity pool. As trade ensues, the value of the liquidity pool may rise or fall. Liquidity providers can redeem their liquidity tokens at any time and get their share of the current liquidity pool paid out in equal values of ETH and tokens. Changes in the composition of the pool from the time a liquidity token is minted to when it is cashed in potentially constitute adverse selection. However, providing liquidity is potentially profitable because each trade generates a fee of 30 basis points that is redeposited into the pool. This fee is the same across all pools and hard-coded as part of the protocol.
Consummating Trade. Suppose a trader wishes to buy the token. In this case, they will deposit ETH into the pool and withdraw the token. Conversely, if they wish to sell the token, they deposit it into the pool and withdraw ETH as payment. The amounts that they have to deposit or withdraw are calculated from the bonding curve that is illustrated in Figure1.
Suppose there areETH andtokens in the pool and a trader wishes to sell the token. They deposit the amount for sale,, into the pool. In return, they receiveand the amount of ETH in the pool drops to.
If the seller was a liquidity trader, the post-trade price in the pool () is now too low, and a potential arbitrageur would enter the market and trade in the opposite direction to return the ratio of ETH to tokens to equilibrium. Note that the liquidity supplier does not benefit from the price impact paid by the liquidity demander—this benefit accrues to the arbitrageur.
For each pool, the constantdepends on the amount of liquidity that has been deposited in the pool up to this point. Further, if more liquidity is posted, the constant increases. (The bonding curve shifts out along a ray.) This is the mechanism through which the market equilibrates.
Pool Size. The price that a trader gets is determined by the bonding curve and the volume of posted liquidity. In particular, the price impact of a marginal increase in the order is. As the liquidity pool grows, the price impact of a fixed order size decreases. Thus, understanding the payoff to liquidity provision and hence the size of each liquidity pool is an important determinant of AMM market quality.
Figure2presents an example of an “order book” that an incoming trader might face. The blue line corresponds to a small pool and the orange line to a larger pool. Because Uniswap has a unique mapping of trading quantity to price, the graph shows the exact amount that is traded at any price. The spread or fixed cost of trading is evident in the interval around the mid-price of 10, for which no quantities can be bought. Clearly, larger orders face worse marginal prices than they would in a typical limit order book.
article.II. Framework:
Consider a market for one asset, with current value. With probability, there is an innovation and the asset is equally likely to jump up or down toor, respectively; otherwise the asset value remains at. Thus,is the proportional innovation. A potential arbitrageur monitors the market and trades whenever profitable; otherwise a liquidity trader, who trades a fixed quantity, arrives. The liquidity trader is equally likely to buy or sell, at any price. At the extreme prices, a trading crowd stands ready to execute orders.
There are two rational, deep pocketed liquidity suppliers who potentially enter the market before the passive trader and supply liquidity that optimally trades off the surplus they can extract from the liquidity trader against the possibility of being “picked off” by an arbitrageur. We focus on the case of two liquidity suppliers as it is the minimum required for competition. These rational liquidity suppliers can privately monitor the market to increase the probability that they will be a monopolist liquidity supplier. The symmetric monitoring cost is. If liquidity supplierchoosesand liquidity supplierchooses, then Nature assignsto be the sole liquidity supplier with probability. This private monitoring captures the idea that liquidity suppliers have an incentive to compete in many different ways, such as co-location or speed.
To simplify the exposition, in the text we describe the case in which the informed trader buys, that is, an innovation leads the asset value to jump up. The case in which the informed trader sells is symmetric. We characterize symmetric equilibria.
The sequence of events in the limit order market is as follows. First, each liquidity supplier chooses their level of private investment. Second, Nature determines if there is one or two limit order submitters, and then each limit order submitter posts orders. In the case of competing liquidity suppliers, one of them is randomly selected to go first. This captures the importance of time priority. After liquidity is posted, Naturedetermines the new asset value. If there is no relative value change, the liquidity trader arrives and trades against the best quote or randomizes if indifferent. If there was a relative value change, the arbitrageur trades if it is profitable (see Figure3).
From the above, it follows that liquidity suppliers may be alone or competing in a market. We characterize their optimal trading strategies in both cases, and then consider their private investment. The amount that the liquidity trader trades is fixed,, and thus this is also the amount that the liquidity suppliers post.
If a liquidity supplier is alone in the market, he will always post a sell price of. Posting at this high price completely mitigates adverse selection, and at the same time extracts maximal surplus from the passive trader.

A sole liquidity supplier makes positive profits, while those in competition make zero profits. Increasing private investment makes it more likely that a liquidity supplier makes positive profits.
The logic in support of competitive liquidity provision is that competition leads to lower prices. However, this is true only if suppliers are competing on price alone. Given the complexity of electronic trading platforms, liquidity suppliers compete in many different ways. Such competition does not necessarily lead to lower prices and may even be inefficient. In the context of our framework, we capture this through liquidity suppliers' private investment to increase the likelihood that they are a monopolist.

The first thing to note is that liquidity provision is not rivalrous and there is no incentive for private investment as there is in the limit order market. The second thing to note is that the AMM requires committed capital.

The parameter restrictions under which equation (16) characterizes pool size are intuitive. If the payoff to liquidity suppliers is too small, then pools are not sustainable. If the payoff to liquidity provision is too large, then arbitrageurs will not find it lucrative to trade on relative price information and will also not trade to ensure that the price implied by the pool corresponds to the traded value of the token. Going forward, we assume that these conditions hold because our empirical focus is on the pools that we observe. Further, as we show in our empirical analysis, the prices implied by Uniswap consistently track traded prices on Binance, which suggests that cross-market arbitrage is profitable.
article.III. Data and Stylized Facts:
DEX are smart contracts deployed mostly on the Ethereum blockchain. Users initiate trade as an Ethereum transaction that sends tokens to a smart contract that in turn calls a function to perform the exchange. The smart contract then sends trade proceeds in the form of the appropriate tokens back. Since transactions on Ethereum are atomic, that is, they either execute completely or fail, there is no settlement risk and users do not have to hand over custody of their digital assets to a third party. In keeping with other DeFi protocols, the source code for many DEX is public and users can verify that the code is not fraudulent. This also allows them to perfectly predict how the smart contract will perform, and more specifically, the terms of trade if they submit an order.
Uniswap is open source with no owner or operator. It was launched in November 2018 at Devcon 4, and in its first release, Uniswap V1 allowed exchange between any ERC20 tokens against Ether (ETH).2Because Uniswap has no designated operator, V1 pools cannot be deleted from the blockchain and exist in parallel to V2 pools. Uniswap V2 was launched on May 18, 2020. The update allows direct trade of any ERC 20 token pairs. If no pool exists for a specific token pair, it can be freely created by invoking the Uniswap factory contract and specifying the token for which a new pool should be created. The factory contract will then deploy a new pool for that specific token on the Ethereum blockchain and enable subsequent trade. As we document below, most V2 pools trade tokens against Wrapped Ether (WETH), which is a ERC 20 representation of Ether (ETH).
To construct our data, we obtain a list of all Uniswap V1 and V2 liquidity pools from the original factory contract transactions. Our sample comprises 105,098 individual liquidity pools, consisting of 2,126 V1 pools and 102,972 V2 pools. We match transactions into and out of these liquidity pools with block-by-block transactions on the Ethereum blockchain. In total we have 94,232,292 transactions on Uniswap between its inception on November 2, 2018, and December 21, 2022. We observe 2,097,032 liquidity injections into a pool, 959,312 withdrawals of liquidity from a pool, and 91,175,948 trades of tokens.
An Ethereum transaction is a set of instructions that are processed as one unit within the same block. Apart from implicit limits on transaction sizes given by Ethereum block size, there is no restriction on how many interactions with Uniswap liquidity pools can be done in one transaction. In our sample, 65.62% of Ethereum transactions have only one interaction with one liquidity pool, another 14.13% have two interactions, and 5,956 transactions or 0.0063% of the sample have 10 or more interactions with a liquidity pool. One transaction interacted with 141 different liquidity pools.3The most complex transaction in terms of number of swaps in our sample has 228 interactions with two different Uniswap liquidity pools.4Uniswap V2 introduced router contracts that can perform complex transactions with one function call. Assume, for example, that a pool exists that trades tokens A and B, another pool trades tokens B and C, and there is no pool to swap A and C. The router contract can be instructed to swap A and C by trading through token B. In our sample, such a transaction would show up as two separate transactions, one for each of the two pools.
It is important to recognize that our analysis is on liquidity supply on both a limit order market and an AMM. Therefore, we do not consider market access fees—either on the limit order market (e.g., co-location, make-take fees) or on the AMM (e.g., gas fees). The latter are analyzed in Capponi and Jia (2021).
Most pools trade against WETH. To compute volume we take the WETH part of the trade and convert it to USD using Binance minute-by-minute data. Most of the remaining pools trade against a USD stablecoin. For those pools we convert the amount traded in the stablecoin to USD. For all remaining pools, we search for all pools in which one of the tokens trades against WETH and we convert it using the prices from the pool with the highest volume. More on the network structure of Uniswap can be found in AppendixD.
TableIprovides an overview of the 10 largest Uniswap V1 and V2 pools by average daily volume in ETH. We exclude pools with less than 5,000 trades and an average poolsize of less than 700 ETH. V1 pools are smaller in terms of both volume and number of trades, mostly because the introduction of V2 coincided with a huge boom in Decentralized Finance and caused most traders and liquidity providers to converge on the new protocol. The largest pool with at least 100 trades in terms of total volume traded is USD Coin (USDC) - Wrapped Ether with an aggregate volume (over all days) of over 43.23 billion USD. The Wrapped Ether - Tether USD pool has the highest number of total trades in our sample, at over 4.61 million trades. The “on average” largest pool in ETH is Wrapped BTC - Wrapped Ether with an average size of around 225,721 ETH.
While some of the pools are very active, many are not: 78,341 pools in our sample have fewer than 100 transactions. Figure6shows the number of trades by pool type. With the release of V2, trading activity in V1 declined. We also observe that for V2 pools, trading against WETH (orange) dominates direct trading of other tokens (red).
Figure7illustrates the trading volume per day. Computing volume is not straightforward in bonding curve markets as attackers often deliberately push markets out of equilibrium. The highest volume in our sample is on March 30, 2021, with a volume of 18.34 billion USD. On that day, a trader moved 5.5 billion USD of a token back and forth between her own wallets. On October 26, 2020, there is volume of over 5.5 million ETH or 2.1 billion USD, linked to an attack on Harvest Finance using a flash swap. A more detailed discussion of this incident and implications for Uniswap volume can be found in AppendixC. Use of flash swaps varies in our sample. Of the 960 days when V2 was deployed, flash swaps occurred on 922 days. The median flash swap volume per day was 164,005 USD, and the maximum was 17.16 billion USD on March 30, 2021. The first flash swap occurred on May 23, 2020.
The largest nonflash swap trade in our sample was on December 17, 2020, when a trader swapped 48,584,947.17 DAI for 342,252.89 WETH  worth about 220.42 million USD at the time as part of an attack on the platform Warp Finance. On June 18, 2020, a trader swapped 100,000.39 WETH (about 23.2 million USD at the time) for 1,695,998.19 Unibomb tokens as part of another exploit.5The median trade size in our sample is 669.95 USD. Moreover 6.70% of trades are below 0.01 ETH and 19.21% are below 100 USD. We provide details on our methodology in AppendixC.
For our subsequent econometric analysis, we purge pools that are very small or that were used for only a few days. Specifically, we drop pools with less than 30 trading days and with less than 100 ETH average balance. The reduced sample has 59,606,977 observations in 1,525 pools.
article.IV. Liquidity Provision:
We have demonstrated empirically that there is heterogeneity in pools. The equilibrium pool size in equation (16) permits us to derive comparative statics.
Proposition 3.Suppose that the current price isand the fee is less than the proportional innovation, or. Then the equilibrium size of a liquidity pool is:


In equilibrium, pool size adjusts so that a pool's per-token fee revenue balances the cost from being picked off by arbitrageurs if the pool posts stale prices. When innovations,, are larger, the informed arbitrageur will ceteris paribus place larger trades, and losses for liquidity providers will therefore be larger. By contrast, fee revenue from the liquidity trader is independent of movements in the fundamental price. This implies that to increase the expected payoff per unit of liquidity, the pool in equilibrium must be smaller. As the pool shrinks, price impact increases and so the informed arbitrageur places a smaller order. These comparative statics highlight how the AMM reaches equilibrium—as the price impact is a deterministic function of the pool size, the size of the pool adjusts to ensure that the liquidity suppliers receive the opportunity cost of their capital.
The equilibrium pool size also decreases in, the probability of an innovation, because this generates informed trading, that is, an arbitrageur trading because of an innovation. Asincreases, the fraction of liquidity traders decreases and the pool is more likely to be picked off by arbitrageurs. The pool size again shrinks in equilibrium, increasing price impact, and reducing the optimal trade size of the informed trader. Figure8shows the pool size as a function ofandfor a numerical example. We summarize our empirical prediction below.
Hypothesis 1.We expect pool size to decrease in the volatility of the exchange rate of the two tokens and increase in uninformed trading.

TableIIpresents our results. Consistent with Hypothesis1, we find that pool size decreases in token price volatility, which is our empirical proxy of the size of the innovation. Higher token price volatility means that liquidity suppliers in the pool lose more when picked off, and thus liquidity providers in equilibrium are compensated with higher per-token fee revenue, which is achieved by reducing the pool size. All three measures of uninformed trading are positively related to pool size as predicted by our model. Our findings hold without fixed effects (columns (1) to (3)) as well as with pool and day fixed effects (columns (4) to (6)).
We focus on two important characteristics of liquidity supply. First, liquidity should be predictable for trades during the course of an average trading day. This reduces uncertainty and risk around the terms of trade. Second, during times of crisis, markets should still be viable, that is, liquidity should be posted.
The strategic incentives of liquidity suppliers in limit order markets can lead to volatility in both cases. One of the characteristics of modern limit order markets is the rapid posting and cancelling of liquidity and quote flickering. This is usually attributed to strategic liquidity provision. (Baruch and Glosten (2013) characterize equilibrium quote flickering in a limit order book.) Further, “flash crashes” or rapid price movements caused by extreme liquidity supply withdrawals have been periodically observed in limit order markets.8In this section, we investigate whether AMM liquidity suppliers are similarly strategic, that is, whether they submit and withdraw liquidity at high frequency. We also examine stability in liquidity during crisis events. Our analysis in SectionV.Bdocuments variation in price impact on a regular trading day, that is, the effect of variation in liquidity supply on the realized terms of trade.
In principle, liquidity provision in an AMM could be as strategic as in a limit order market. To investigate if liquidity providers engage in high-frequency strategic liquidity provision, we track liquidity providers by the wallet address where they store their liquidity tokens. Of our 94.2 million observations, we find only 33,256 events where the same person deposited (withdrew) liquidity and withdrew (deposited) liquidity in the same pool within 50 blocks.9Most of these withdrawals or additions are small, the median size is 1,797 USD or 2.76% of the pool, which has a very small impact on trading costs.10We conclude that liquidity providers on Uniswap V2 do not engage in high-frequency strategic liquidity provision and withdrawals.
In our sample, 1.02% of all the interactions with Uniswap liquidity pools are withdrawals. Further, these withdrawals are not coincident with extreme market events. For example, on May 19, 2021, ETH dropped from over 3,400 USD to 2,014 USD, a 41% decline. To put this in perspective, on October 19, 1987, the S&P 500 index dropped by only 20.5%. The orange area in Figure9shows minute-by-minute pricing data from Binance. We examine liquidity withdrawals from the large USD stablecoin pools that trade against ETH: USDT, USDC, and DAI. The blue line in Panel A shows aggregate withdrawals from these pools in percent of pre-event pool size. When Ether reached its lowest price, only about 2% of liquidity was withdrawn and users could trade with minimal price impact. Most withdrawals of liquidity happen around 53 minutes or 245 blocks after the big decline, when prices have already recovered. Overall, only 17% of the liquidity gets withdrawn in this extreme price movement.
Two factors mitigate liquidity providers' incentives to withdraw. First, increasing fee revenue partially offsets any losses that liquidity providers experience. The green line in Panel A shows cumulative fee revenue during the plot period. Between 7 a.m. and 3 p.m. UTC liquidity providers collected almost 3 million USD in fees. Trading volume and thus fee revenue might be high in periods of strong price movements because levered traders unwind their positions or are involuntarily liquidated (Lehar and Parlour (2023)) or because arbitrageurs take advantage of pricing differences across pools. Second, when liquidity providers want to withdraw liquidity, they have to pay gas fees on the blockchain to compensate the miner for the computational cost of processing the transactions. Withdrawing liquidity requires the liquidity provider's share of the fee revenue to be computed, which renders withdrawals computationally complex and thus more expensive relative to a swap. Panel B shows the median gas cost of a liquidity withdrawal. Gas costs spike as ETH drops, making it uneconomical for many liquidity providers to withdraw.
Our model is single period and does not speak to pool dynamics. However, from Figure8we can see that the optimal pool size decreases in the innovation of price, and thus liquidity gets withdrawn when prices move a lot. While not explicitly modeled, it is clear that high withdrawal costs would mitigate this effect. From the discussion in SectionIV.A, we know that pool size increases in fee revenue. Higher fee revenue therefore also mitigates the effect of higher volatility.
To test more broadly if high fee revenue and high gas fees coincide with periods of high volatility, we collect daily token returns and fee revenue for all liquidity pools with at least 10,000 USD in inventory and that are active for at least 30 days. We also collect average gas prices for all transactions that interact with a Uniswap liquidity pool. AppendixFdetails our gas cost estimates. Variables are winsorized at the 95% level. We regress gas fees and fee revenue on the absolute return of the exchange rate in the pool as well as on a dummy set equal to one if the absolute daily return is more than 10% to measure extreme price movements. To interpret our regressions, note that times of high price movements are necessarily times of high adverse selection costs, as these are the times that arbitrageurs trade. TableIIIpresents our findings. We find that on days of extreme market movements, gas costs of liquidity withdrawals are about 1.71 USD or 10% more expensive and pools earn 17.7 USD more in fees. Both gas costs and fee revenue increase in the absolute return.
Rapid withdrawals of liquidity supply as we highlighted in our model are a feature of modern limit order markets. Further, such withdrawals have contributed to flash crashes. By contrast, liquidity suppliers in Uniswap pools are very stable. In so far as deep and constant liquidity is socially beneficial, the design of the AMM is effective. We reiterate that Ethereum gas fees act as a commitment device for liquidity suppliers to remain in pools.
article.V. Trading Costs Comparison between a Li:
To compare the limit order market with an AMM, we focus on the total trading cost for a liquidity trader, which consists of fees and price impact.11Recall that each liquidity supplier has undertaken private investment and is a monopolist with probability. The liquidity trader thus faces a monopolist with probability, which we denote by, and enjoys competitive liquidity provision with probability.
Proposition 4.On a limit order market, the expected cost to the liquidity trader per unit is

On an active AMM pool, the expected cost to the liquidity trader per unit is


Trading costs on the limit order market are higher if the liquidity supplier is a monopolist and if there is more informed trading in the market. As noted, the payoffs to liquidity suppliers on the DEX are shared pro rata and so the equilibrium liquidity supply and hence the cost to the liquidity trader are determined by the trade-off between picking off risk and fees. These differences mean that the limit order market does not always dominate the AMM. When informed trading is low, the liquidity pool is large and price impact is low and thus cheaper for the liquidity trader. The price impact in the limit order market does not decrease in informed trading to the same extent because liquidity provision is not as competitive.12
Our model also implies that price impact in limit order markets is more volatile because the trader does not know ex ante if he will face a monopolist liquidity provider or a competitive market. Since monopolist prices differ substantially from competitive ones, we expect price impact to vary. For AMM, price impact is known ex ante and can be very low when the pool is sufficiently large.
Proposition 5.The limit order market does not dominate the AMM.


Figure10depicts the region of the parameter space in which total trading costs for the liquidity trader in the liquidity pool are lower than in the limit order market. The left panel shows that an AMM is the better trading venue for the liquidity trader when either volatility,, or the probability of an innovation and associated informed trading,, are sufficiently small. In those cases the pool is large and the liquidity trader can trade without much price impact. The right panel shows that for a given probability of an innovation, liquidity pools dominate when the trade size of the liquidity trader is neither too small, which generates low fee revenue, nor too large.
Our definition of AMM transaction costs includes the fee, while our definition of transaction costs on the limit order market does not include any fees (make/take fees). Note that the trading cost on the AMM differs from price impact. This is because on the AMM, each unit trades at a different marginal price. The equivalent notion on a limit order book would be the total amount paid as an order walked the book. Empirically, we observe the price after each transaction (the marginal price) and not the total amount paid.
There are three additional reasons why a comprehensive empirical analysis of the differences in costs is challenging. First, as we discuss in SectionV.Bbelow, assets traded on an AMM differ from those traded on a CEX. In addition to the nonfungibility of assets, trader sorting across the two venues is likely. Second, to estimate price impact on a limit order market, quote data are important.13This is because aggressive orders always cross the spread and pay it. Finally, order submission strategies on both venues differ, as there is an incentive to split orders on limit markets. Based on Proposition5and with these caveats in mind, we formulate the following empirical hypotheses.
Hypothesis 2.We expect AMMs to have lower price impact relative to the limit order market for low volatility, more noise trading, and medium trade sizes.


Hypothesis 3.We expect AMMs to have lower variation in price impact relative to the limit order market.

Our framework does not incorporate market access fees. However, various papers such as Capponi and Jia (2021) and Barbon and Ranaldo (2021) emphasize gas fees as an important determinant of Uniswap market quality. Fully quantifying the difference between on-chain markets (such as Uniswap) and off-chain markets (such as Binance) is complex. First, tokens on-chain and off-chain differ in custody risk and use value. Second, off-chain venues have their own market access fees and in some cases make-take fees.
Binance, as with all centralized crypto exchanges, retains custody of traded assets. As the recent collapse of FTX illustrates, this custody risk is not insignificant. Uniswap, in contrast, is noncustodial, meaning that the user can initiate a trade directly out of their personal wallet and keep custody of traded assets until they are swapped in an atomic transaction.
Uniswap traded tokens can also be used in other DeFi protocols, for example, for yield farming or as collateral. Binance-based tokens have to be transferred out of Binance custody into a user's wallet before they can be used in other protocols. Similarly, any tokens that a user wants to trade on the exchange must be transferred out the user's personal wallet into the exchange wallet, and the exchange has to give the user credit for these assets in their own internal ledger before they can be traded. Both steps incur transaction costs.
In short, owning a token on Binance has different attributes than owning a token on-chain. The difference in value should account for both the opportunity cost of being off-chain and having access to the different protocols and the direct cost of being exposed to Binance credit risk.
We can quantify blockchain transaction costs by comparing a swap transaction on a DEX to two token transfers, which would be required to move a token into and out of a CEX. Gas costs are the product of gas amount and gas price. The former is determined by the computational complexity of the transaction and the latter is set by the user. We collect blockchain data for both, and present the details of the estimate in AppendixF. The average incremental fee of trading on a DEX is 0.94 cents, while the median incremental fee is 0.22 cents. On some days, the incremental fee can be very high. Indeed, the maximum in our sample is 11.57 USD on May 11, 2021, when ETH first broke through 4,000 USD. For the latter part of our sample, since January 2021, the median incremental fee was 1.33 USD. Finally, note that our calculation does not include any withdrawal fees that an exchange may charge. Binance, for example, charges token-specific fees that are hard to include in a comparison. Binance charges a fee of 0.005 ETH for ETH withdrawals to the Ethereum blockchain, which at the time of writing would be equivalent to 8.50 USD.
To compare Uniswap to traditional exchanges, we collect minute-by-minute trading data from Binance (international), one of the largest crypto-exchanges by volume. Many of the 2,148 token pairs listed on Binance trade against fiat currencies. We find 166 token pairs that trade on both Uniswap and Binance, but many of these pairs are infrequently traded. We eliminate all pairs with an average daily volume of less than 10 ETH on either market, less than 90 trading days, and an average daily Uniswap pool size of less than 10 ETH. We also eliminate all observations in the first 90 days of the initiation of trading on either venue and where the pricing error between the two venues is more than 10%. We treat WETH and ETH as identical given the easy and cheap conversion. We end up with 43 token pairs that are cross-listed on Uniswap and Binance.
Figure12shows intraday prices of the USDC/ETH pair on one day, October 21, 2020. The pattern is typical for most days in our sample. Binance prices often lead Uniswap prices and, as expected, Binance prices are more volatile than those on Uniswap.
Following Proposition5, we expect some Uniswap pools to have lower price impact relative to a limit order market such as Binance. We define price impact as the absolute change in price over the change in quantity traded in a one-minute interval. We then average price impact measures over a day. Figure13shows our measures for price impact for the USDC/ETH pair. We can see that price impact on Binance almost always exceeds that on Uniswap. Binance's price impact also varies over time while the price impact on Uniswap stays constant and is negligible once the pool has reached an equilibrium size (in September 2020). We also compute the theoretical price impact for Uniswap, which we derive analytically from the bonding curve in equation (1) assuming zero fees. We find that our analytical measure of price impact on Uniswap (green line) corresponds closely to our empirical measure (orange line).
We observe a large drop in the price impact on Binance as of March 2022. On March 25, Binance integrated PancakeSwap into their trading app. PancakeSwap is a DEX similar to Uniswap running on the Binance Smart Chain (BSC). Binance simultaneously introduced the Binance DeFi wallet which allows users of the CEX to open a wallet and use the DeFi application on BSC out of the app.14The integration of a DEX into the Binance trading app is associated with a substantial drop in price impact on Binance.
To control for the PancakeSwap integration into Binance, we set the dummyto one after March 25, 2022. We expect Binance price impact to align closer with that of Uniswap after the integration of a Uniswap clone into Binance. Trade size is the combined volume of Binance and Uniswap in ETH divided by the combined number of trades on Binance and Uniswap. As in the numerical example in Figure10, we expect AMMs to have lower price impact for middle trade sizes, and thus rPI to be U-shaped in trade size. Our measure of noise trading is the daily volume of trades that are immediately reversed on Uniswap measured in ETH. Standard errors are clustered by pool and by day. Models that include the pancake dummy only have pool fixed effects. For robustness, we run the same regression with pool and day fixed effects without the dummy.
TableIVpresents our findings. We first note that the intercept is positive and significant, which demonstrates that price impact on Binance is on average higher than on Uniswap. The DEX integration on Binance as measured by the Pancake dummy is associated with a significant drop in Binance price impact relative to Uniswap. Consistent with this Hypothesis2, we find that the price impact on Binance decreases in volatility relative to Uniswap. As predicted, our measure of relative price impact is first increasing and then decreasing in trade size. Higher reversal volume, which proxies for less informed trading, is associated with higher relative price impact on Binance.
The last two columns of TableIVpresent results for the subsample before the PancakeSwap integration in column (8) and after in column (9). We find that most variables become insignificant after the PancakeSwap integration. The intercept is smaller but still significant post-integration, documenting that Uniswap has lower price impact in both subsamples.
The relative attractiveness of AMM attracts traders to DEX. Figure14shows the trading volume of the USDC/ETH pair on Binance and Uniswap. We can see that trading volume is remarkably correlated across the two markets, which is surprising given that tokens have to be moved back and forth through on-chain transactions between the two markets. We can also see that Uniswap gains market share over time and eventually more trading happens on Uniswap relative to Binance. Toward the end of the sample, trading volume on Uniswap V2 declines, most likely due to the fact that volume moves to Uniswap V3.
article.VI. Conclusion:
In 1971, Fischer Black wrote two articles forFinancial Analyst's Journalspeculating on whether computers or “automation” could ever replace human interaction in financial markets (Black (1971a,1971b)). In these papers, he argued that market liquidity is constrained by the size of a market maker's inventory and suggested that a solution would be to have more direct participation from other market participants.
The Uniswap experiment does not merely increase the supply of liquidity by relaxing market makers' inventory constraints. It also changes how the benefits and costs of liquidity provision are shared among market participants. In a limit order market, the exchange sets price and time priority, which effectively determine the interaction of liquidity demand and supply. Price impact in a limit order market is the endogenous outcome of the interaction. By contrast, on an AMM, the price impact is programmatically determined by a bonding curve. We note, however, that this impact is conditional on the size of the pool. Thus, the equilibrium price impact arises as the pool size adjusts, so that liquidity suppliers trade off potential adverse selection against fee revenue.
The automated Uniswap protocol has clearly been successful. Further, in July 2021, Swarm, a Berlin-based DeFi company, announced that it was launching an AMM that was fully licensed by the German regulator, BaFin. We have demonstrated both theoretically and empirically that one of the reasons for the uptake of these liquidity-sharing protocols is that pools adjust to trade off the benefits and costs of liquidity provision. Further, compared with a CEX, there are some token pairs for which the AMM provides liquidity more efficiently.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong

article.Appendix B: Data:
Blockchain data are publicly available. Anyone can download and run an Ethereum node, which collects all blockchain transactions. Transactions and interactions with smart contracts such as Uniswap leave log entries on the blockchain, which can be accessed through an API from the Ethereum node or bought from a data provider. At the time of writing, the Ethereum blockchain has about 3.8 billion log entries. We bought access to raw Ethereum log entries from Covalent. Not all data providers are of equal quality. Aggregate data such as periodic snapshots can miss important information as DEX liquidity can change substantially within a block or even within a transaction. Such intra-block changes will be missed by snapshots but captured by our log-level data as we can reconstruct all transactions within the block in the sequence that they are executed. We are confident in our data as we conducted many robustness checks and comparisons with data obtained directly from our own Ethereum archive node.
Smart contracts are essentially small computer programs that execute in the Ethereum Virtual Machine. The code can generate log events and store the event and some data on the blockchain. The Uniswap smart contract generates several events every time a user interacts with it to deposit, withdraw, or trade tokens or when a new pool is created. We filter over 200 million relevant log events and reverse engineer from the data stored with these log events how users interacted with the smart contracts.
FigureB1shows one transaction in our sample16on Etherscan, a commonly used web interface to examine Ethereum transactions. We see the transaction hash, that it was mined in block 13,738,106 on December 4, 2021 at 5:47:46 UTC time. The user offered a gas price of 492.5 Gwei. FigureB2shows the relevant log entries. The swap log event shows the two parties (one can be a router contract) and the amounts of tokens that flow in and out of the pool. The associated sync event shows the balance for each token in the liquidity pool. We carefully match swap and sync events as well as the minting and burning of liquidity tokens. Log events are not always recorded in the same sequence.
article.Appendix C: Measuring Volume:
Dollar volume is a natural volume measure on U.S. exchanges, and there is typically a recognized common price. On Uniswap, the U.S. dollar is not the numeraire; most of the trading in the Uniswap system is against WETH or USD stablecoins. Further, the existence of flash loans and flash swaps during so-called oracle attacks means that there can be large price discrepancies across trading venues.
To compute volume, we take the WETH part of the trade and convert it to USD using Binance minute-by-minute data. We use the Binance price because the internal Uniswap price is sometimes purposely distorted. As we have indicated, large trades such as oracle attacks can push relative token prices out of equilibrium. Most of the remaining pools trade against a USD stablecoin. For those pools, we convert stablecoin to USD. For all remaining pools, we search for all pools where one of the tokens trades against WETH and convert using the prices from the pool with the highest volume. We then use external data from Binance to convert the ETH to USD.
As a concrete example of price distortions caused by oracle attacks, a December 17, 2020, trade against Warp Finance is in our sample.17The initiator borrowed about 200 million USD in flash loans from Uniswap and dYdX to manipulate the DAI/ETH price by trading 48.58 million DAI against 342,252 WETH. DAI is a USD stablecoin, so one side of the trade corresponds roughly to 48 million USD. One ETH was worth about 643 USD at the time, making the WETH side of the trade worth about 220 million USD. The relatively complex trade involved depositing DAI and WETH into a liquidity pool and then using the liquidity tokens as collateral in Warp Finance. Finally, using a large trade to distort the DAI/WETH price led the Warp code to overvalue the deposited collateral.
Smart contract nuances aside, this trade was designed to distort the DAI/WETH price, which means that using contemporaneous prices may distort the volume. Thus, in the example above, we would measure the volume as 220 million USD. Our approach does not systematically inflate volume because oracle attacks can happen in both directions and all trades that push prices out of equilibrium also have an opposing trade that brings prices back to equilibrium.
Many other contracts such as lending platforms rely on DEX as price feeds or oracles to determine, for example, the value of the collateral in relation to the face value of an outstanding loan. Attackers can exploit poorly written code of such lending platforms for financial gain. Typically, large trades are used to move prices in the bonding curve market that the lending contract uses as oracle, making the smart contract believe that the collateral is very valuable. The attacker then borrows against the collateral, brings the price on the bonding curve market back to equilibrium, and walks away from the now undercollateralized loan. One such attack happened on Harvest Finance, a yield farming cooperative similar to a floating net asset value money market fund. The user could deposit tokens with Harvest Finance in return for fAsset tokens (e.g., depositors of USDC receive a fUSDC token). The underlying tokens were invested in high-yielding liquidity pools and the revenues are shared with the holders of fAsset tokens.
On October 26, 2020, an attacker borrowed 18 million USDT and 50 million USDC on Uniswap and converted over 17 million USDT to USDC on curve.fi (an AMM similar to Uniswap that specialized in trading stablecoins).18This temporarily increased the price of USDC in the curve.fi pool, which is used as a price oracle for Harvest Finance. The attacker then deposited USDC with Harvest in exchange for an inflated number of fUSDC tokens. Specifically, the price manipulation caused the price of fUSDC to temporarily drop to 0.9712 from 0.98 before the attack. Then the attacker changed 17 million USDC back to USDT on curve.fi and sold his fUSDC tokens back to Harvest Finance at 0.9833, as Harvest Finance's smart contract updated the price based on the new information from curve.fi. The net profit of this attack was 619,408 USDC. The hacker then repeated the process 17 times and also attacked other Harvest Finance pools for a total profit of USD 24 million.19
Transactions are finalized if they are incorporated onto the Ethereum blockchain, and anything that happens in the same transaction effectively happens at the same time. For this reason, flash swaps were introduced in Uniswap V2. This feature allows a user to borrow any amount up to the total liquidity available in a pool, only if the whole sum gets returned in the same Ethereum transaction. Because an Ethereum transaction is atomic, that is, is executed either in its entirety or not at all, there is no credit risk for lenders as the loan is both originated and repaid in the same transaction.20In our data, 735,644 transactions combine liquidity additions or removals with swaps or flash swaps. To be consistent with websites like uniswap.info, in this paper, we include flash swaps in volume computations. Liquidity providers earn a fee identical to the one on regular token swaps that is based on the gross amount of the flash loan regardless of whether the repayment is the same token that was borrowed. For liquidity providers, flash swaps offer a risk-free way to earn earn higher fees.
Data Issues:
We also observe some transactions that trade tokens back and forth without apparent reason. For example, in one transaction on March 30, 2021, somebody created a new Uniswap exchange for a token named SCAMMY, borrowed WETH worth 220 million USD in a flash loan on dYdX, injected half as liquidity to the pool, and then traded 50 times the other half of the funds back and forth for a total volume of USD 5 billion.21The trader then withdrew the liquidity and repaid the flash loan. There is no obvious profit motive for this transaction. One possibility is that the trader tried to generate high fee revenue to place the token in a leading position at one of the yield farming websites in order to attract investment to this scam token (although naming the token scammy is not helpful for this purpose). We include such events in graphs and summary statistics. For our econometric analysis, we winsorize the data and thus eliminate such outliers.
Ticker symbols on Uniswap are not protected. Anyone can create a token and assign the ticker symbol of a popular token like WETH or USDC. Tokens are uniquely identified by their address, for example, 0xc02aaa39b223fe8d0a0e5c4f27ead9083c756cc2 for WETH, which is not easy to work with. Most people therefore use tickers and are exposed to copycat tokens. TableC1lists fraudulent versions of popular tokens. We can see that, for example, Yearn Finance (YFI) has 17 copycat tokens that use the same ticker symbol. A total of 330 transactions were done on Uniswap with copycat tokens, which is small compared to 284,973 transactions in the legitimate token. Overall, we find that the amount of trading in fake tokens is small and will not affect our findings.
article.Appendix D: Stylized Facts on DEXs:
In contrast to traditional exchanges, Uniswap liquidity pools are not certified. There are no listing requirements. A consequence of this is that some of the token pools are purposely misleading. For example, five different tokens in our sample share the same ticker symbol USDC. A naive user who does not verify the relevant smart contract addresses could be tricked into buying a worthless coin with the same ticker.22Overall trading activity in these fraudulent pools is limited and will not affect our results. We provide detailed information on fake tokens in AppendixC.
FigureD1presents the network of pools between all tokens that are part of the 50 largest pools by volume. The thickness of each edge corresponds to the trading volume between the tokens. We can see that Wrapped Ether (WETH) takes a central position in the Uniswap network. For our full sample of 101,884 tokens in 105,098 pools we find that 99,863 pools, or 95.02%, trade directly against ETH or WETH. The second highest number of tokens, 2,148, trade against USDT. The highest volume and the most connections are between WETH and USD stablecoins such as USDT, USDC, and DAI. 5,244 tokens, or 4.99% of tokens, trade directly against these three stablecoins. We note in passing that the Uniswap network has a core-periphery structure similar to many other financial networks. We find that 99,491 tokens, or 97.65% of tokens, trade only against one other token.

As with any passive liquidity provider, the Uniswap pools present a free option to the market. That is, if the quantities in the pool are such that the terms of trade differ from the true value, arbitrageurs are more likely to pick off stale liquidity. This logic is reflected in the previous example. However, liquidity-demanding trades are always valuable. The liquidity suppliers receive a fee for the liquidity-demanding order and also receive a fee when equilibrium is replenished by arbitrage traders. They only face potential losses if there has been a permanent value change in the token.
If the price of a token moves away from the fundamental value because of a large order, an arbitrageur will initiate an offsetting trade and bring the mid-price of the exchange back to the fundamental value. Such short-term deviations from the fundamental price of a token are beneficial to liquidity holders. In a pool without fees, liquidity providers will gain zero on such a trading pattern. Trades are always priced in such a way that the amount of ETH and tokens are on the bonding curve before and after any trade (see equation (1)). Thus, a move fromtoand then back towill leave the liquidity providers at exactly the same point they started from. Many crypto-traders refer to gains or losses while the pool is off equilibrium at valueasimpermanent loss. With positive fees, liquidity traders benefit from such short-term deviations as they collect a proportional fee for both trades. In our empirical analysis, we estimate such short-term deviations from fundamental value as reversals.
Finally, even though arbitrageurs replenish the liquidity pool after a large price movement, they pay a fee for doing so. This differs from a traditional limit order book in which liquidity is replenished by rivalrous liquidity suppliers.
Example E2.Continue Example 1, case (a), and suppose that an arbitrageur brings the price closer to the fundamental value. Assume that the arbitrageur can buy tokens at the fundamental value of 10, sells them to the pool at price, and chooses the optimum amount of tokens t to sell to the pool to maximize profit,. By sendingtokens to the pool, he will obtainETH in return, resulting in price. Solving for the optimalthat maximizes the arbitrageur's profit, we find, which is smaller than the amount of token sold by the pool in ExampleE1because (i) the invarianthas changed after the first trade due to the fee revenue and (ii) fees make it optimal for the arbitrageur to sell a smaller amount back to the pool. It is easy to verify that without fees, that is,, the invariant doesnot change after the first trade, the arbitrageur would sell exactly the same amount of tokens back to the pool that the pool sold in the previous trade, and the new mid-price of the pool would exactly equal the fundamental value. With fees, however, the arbitrageur optimally sellstokens to the pool, for which he receives 9.836 ETH, leaving the pool with a new balance of 100.164 ETH and 9.98904 token. The new pool mid-price is 10.0274, which deviates slightly from the fundamental value of 10. Liquidity providers value their token holdings at the fundamental value of 10 and hold a total of, which is higher than their initial investment of 200.
article.Appendix F: Gas Fees:
Gas fees are the transaction costs on Ethereum blockchain. In the context of our paper, gas fees are important for two reasons. First, they are one quantifiable component of the cost difference between CEX and DEX. Second, gas costs that are high in times of market stress provide incentives for liquidity providers not to withdraw.
Gas is quoted in units of Gwei, where 1 Gwei isETH. The amount of gas used for a specific transaction consists of a predictable and state-contingent component. The predictable component comes from the organization of the Ethereum Virtual Machine. Any transaction comprises a known number of individual low-level computational steps. Fixed gas amounts are assigned to each type of operation and are independent of market conditions—the total gas used for a transaction is simply the sum of each computational step. In addition, users offer an amount of ETH per unit of gas consumed by the program, the so-called gas price, as an incentive to the miners to be included. The total gas fee is then the gas amount multiplied by the gas price. The gas price varies with market conditions and demand for transactions. Given a market price of Ethereum, the gas fee can be translated into USD.
We note three stylized facts that are relevant for our analysis. First, the average gas fee is low; second, the state-contingent fee can be high; and finally, the incremental fee relative to trading on Binance is low. Intuitively, the high state-contingent fee acts as a precommitment not to withdraw liquidity in stressed market events.
To compare gas fees on a CEX and a DEX, we compare a swap transaction to two token transfers, which would be required to move a token in and out of a CEX. To measure gas costs, we pick a sample of 200,000 blocks starting from block 11,668,958. Gas prices are recorded per transaction and each transaction can contain multiple swaps, multiple transfers, or other interactions with DeFi platforms. To generate a conservative estimate, we pick the 10 lowest gas amounts for transactions that contain swaps or transfers. These transactions are most likely to contain a “minimalist” transaction, that is, for example, just a simple token transfer. We manually verify on Etherscan that this is indeed the case. In these data, a Uniswap V2 swap uses on average 49.973 units of gas and a transfer uses 19.830 units of gas.23A swap therefore uses on average 10.313 more gas units than the two token transfers that would be required to move tokens to and from a CEX. Using a similar approach, we measure the gas amount of a liquidity withdrawal as 97,686 gas units. Liquidity withdrawals are the most expensive interactions with Uniswap as the smart contract has to compute and transfer the liquidity provider's share of the fee revenue.
Using average gas prices can be misleading for two reasons. First, gas prices have a fat right tail and averages might be driven by outliers. Second, market conditions might make it very lucrative to use one DeFi protocol over another at a given time or users might have different preferences to get their transaction included in a timely manner. To best capture preferences of Uniswap users, on each day, we compute the median gas price for all Uniswap transactions, multiply that value by the incremental gas amount of 10.312 gas units, and convert that value to USD. FigureF1illustrates our findings. The average incremental fee is 0.94 cents; the median incremental fee is 0.22 cents. On some days the incremental fee can be very high. Indeed, the maximum in our sample is 11.57 USD on May 11, 2021, when ETH first broke through 4,000 USD. For the second part of our sample, since January 2021, the median incremental fee was 1.33 USD.
Finally, note that our calculation does not include any withdrawal fees that an exchange may charge. Binance, for example, charges token-specific fees that are hard to include in a comparison. Binance charges a fee of 0.005 ETH for ETH withdrawals to the Ethereum blockchain, which at the time of writing would be equivalent to 8.5 USD.

AItitle: 去中心化交易所流动性革命：AMM资金池动态调节实证
AIsummary: 这篇论文聚焦于区块链金融领域，探讨去中心化交易所Uniswap的自动化做市商（AMM）机制如何解决传统限价订单簿市场的流动性供给问题。传统金融市场依赖主动定价的流动性供应商竞争，存在高频交易、策略性撤资和价格波动风险。而Uniswap通过创新设计，将流动性供给与价格发现分离：流动性提供者被动存入资产形成资金池，交易价格由预设的“绑定曲线”机械计算，套利者负责修正价格偏差以平衡市场。  研究团队分析了近亿次Uniswap交易数据，发现AMM在低波动资产和中等交易规模下显著降低了价格冲击成本，且流动性供给更稳定——尤其在极端市场事件中，手续费收益与区块链交易成本共同抑制了流动性撤资。相比传统市场，AMM通过“资金池规模动态调节”实现均衡：池规模越大，单位交易的价格影响越小，但需权衡套利风险与手续费收益。这一机制使AMM成为某些资产类别更高效的流动性解决方案，重新定义了市场参与者间的价值分配逻辑。
AItitle-cn: “DeFi突破：科学家破解以太坊飙升的天然气费用代码”
AIsummary-cn: 以下是一句话对论文的总结：本文研究了去中心化金融（DeFi）领域，旨在解决与基于以太坊的平台上的交易相关的高gas费用问题，提出了一种估算和比较集中式交易所（CEX）和去中心化交易所（DEX）之间gas费用的方法，特别是Uniswap V2。这是一篇250字的更长的论文总结：本文重点关注DeFi领域，由于其有可能使金融服务的获取民主化，该领域近年来受到了广泛关注。然而，DeFi平台面临的主要挑战之一是与基于以太坊的平台上的交易相关的高天然气费用。这些费用对于小额交易来说可能非常昂贵，使用户难以参与DeFi活动。之前的研究试图使用各种方法估算和比较CEX和DEX之间的天然气费用。然而，这些方法存在局限性，并不能全面了解天然气费用情况。作者提出了一种新方法，该方法考虑了Uniswap V2上掉期交易所需的增量气体量，与将代币移入和移出CEX所需的两次代币转移相比。作者收集了Uniswap V2交易中使用的天然气价格和金额的数据，并计算了所有Uniswap交易的天然气中值价格。然后，他们将该值乘以增量天然气量，并将其转换为美元。结果表明，平均增量费用较低，但在某些日子可能会很高。作者还指出，国家或有费用可以作为在压力市场事件中不提取流动性的预先承诺。总的来说，本文提供了一种新的方法来估算和比较CEX和DEX之间的天然气费用，这可以帮助用户做出明智的决定，决定使用哪个平台进行他们的DeFi活动。
Highlights:
学术要点 1：流动性池均衡条件（Liquidity Pool Equilibrium Conditions） 通过池规模动态调整平衡套利风险与费用收益，当资产波动率降低或非信息交易量增加时，流动性池规模扩大。|||
学术要点 2：价格影响确定性（Deterministic Price Impact） 基于恒定乘积公式的机械计算机制，使交易价格冲击可精确预测，区别于传统订单簿的策略性报价行为。|||
学术要点 3：选择风险（Picking-off Risk） 流动性提供者因套利者利用价格滞后进行再平衡操作导致的损失，驱动流动性池规模内生决定。|||
学术要点 4：Gas费用承诺机制（Gas Fee Commitment Device） 区块链交易费用在极端市场条件下形成退出壁垒，抑制流动性提供者的策略性撤回行为。|||
学术要点 5：流动性供给非竞争性（Non-Rivalrous Liquidity Provision） 按比例共享收益的机制消除流动性提供者间的价格竞争，形成与传统订单簿不同的激励结构。
